desc: Introduced from India. Parthenogenetic — all females, no males needed for reproduction. Bright green stick insect up to 4 inches. On ivy and ornamental plants in LA gardens. Popular lab animal and classroom pet. Nocturnal herbivore.

fm.Behavior: Parthenogenetic (no males); slow-moving; excellent crypsis; drops to ground when disturbed
fm.Habitat: Urban gardens — ivy, privet, roses, ornamentals

hp: Stick insect — cryptic herbivore on shrub foliage. Twig mimic. Prey for birds.

desc: Western stick insect — native stick insect of chaparral and coastal sage scrub. Brown, shorter antennae than introduced phasmids. Feeds on chamise and other native shrubs. Cryptic — looks exactly like a dead twig. Distinguished from introduced Carausius by brown color and habitat.

fm.Color: Brown to gray-brown; twig-like; short antennae
fm.Behavior: Nocturnal; hangs motionless on chaparral branches by day

hp: Coastal sage scrub and chaparral. Associated with Black Sage. In chaparral habitat. Cryptically colored.

desc: California mantis — LA's native praying mantis. Green or brown morphs. Smaller and more delicate than introduced Chinese or European mantids. Males fly to lights at night. Ambush predator on flowers and foliage. Declining — outcompeted by larger introduced species.

fm.Color: Green or brown (dimorphic); wings shorter than abdomen in ♀
fm.Behavior: Ambush predator; males fly to lights; cannibalistic mating
fm.Diet: Predator — any insect it can catch

hp: LA's native mantis — declining due to competitive displacement by introduced Tenodera sinensis (Hurd 1999). Green or brown morphs.

desc: Introduced. The classic 'praying mantis.' Larger than native species. Green with black spot inside forelegs (diagnostic).

fm.Color: Green (rarely brown); black eyespot on inner foreleg (diagnostic)
fm.Behavior: Ambush predator; sold commercially for biocontrol
fm.Season: May–Nov; egg cases on stems overwinter

hp: Ambush predator of flying insects. Beneficial garden predator.

desc: Introduced from Asia, now LA's largest mantis at up to 4 inches. Tan-green with distinctive green lateral forewing stripe. Voracious generalist predator — occasionally takes hummingbirds and small lizards. Sold as 'beneficial insect' but may harm native insects.

fm.Color: Tan to green; green lateral wing stripe
fm.Behavior: Ambush predator; catches hummingbirds at feeders occasionally
fm.Diet: Predator — large insects, occasionally small vertebrates
fm.Season: May–Nov; large egg cases on branches

hp: Generalist predator of insects and other arthropods. Visited by/prey for hummingbirds.

desc: Bordered mantis — desert mantis similar to S. californica but typically in drier, hotter habitats at desert edge. Green or brown. Males with longer wings than females. Ambush predator. Distinguished from California Mantis by habitat and subtle structural differences.

fm.Color: Green or brown; often more brownish than S. californica

hp: Praying mantis — ambush predator of flying insects. Beneficial garden predator. Desert habitat.

desc: LA's smallest and most easily overlooked mantis at barely 25mm. Gray-brown, ground-dwelling, found walking on soil and rocks rather than on vegetation. Cryptic against sand and gravel. Native. Desert and open scrub habitats.

fm.Behavior: Ground-dwelling; runs rather than flies; easily missed due to size
fm.Habitat: Rocky ground, sandy soil, desert scrub

hp: Praying mantis — ambush predator of flying insects. Beneficial garden predator. Desert habitat.

desc: Common garden earwig with rear pincers. Nocturnal omnivore. Female guards eggs — rare maternal care in insects.
hp: Predator of earwigs and soil-dwelling insects. Preys on earwig.

desc: Small wingless stick insect on Ceanothus. Classic subject of speciation research. Some populations parthenogenetic.
hp: On ceanothus (california lilac) — native shrub specialist. Timematidae.

desc: Small brown cockroach increasingly replacing the oriental cockroach across SoCal. Males can fly; females have reduced wings. Prefers outdoor habitats — leaf litter, mulch, irrigated areas. Originally from Central Asia. Less of an indoor pest than German cockroach.
hp: Part of local invertebrate food web. In leaf litter.

desc: Small tan cockroach (12-15mm), the world's most successful indoor pest insect. Rapid reproduction (30-40 eggs per ootheca, 4-6 oothecae per female). Exclusively indoor — cannot survive outdoors in most climates. Highly resistant to many insecticides.

fm.Color: Tan with two dark pronotal stripes

hp: Most successful indoor pest insect globally (Schal & Hamilton 1990). Rapid reproduction

desc: Large reddish-brown cockroach to 40mm. Despite name, of African origin. Called 'waterbug' in SoCal. Prefers warm moist environments — sewers, basements, restaurants. Can fly short distances in warm weather. Fastest running insect relative to size.
hp: Ecological role from description context

desc: Silvery wingless fish-shaped insect in bathrooms and closets. Ancient lineage — predates dinosaurs by 100 million years. Eats paper, glue, starch.

fm.Behavior: Nocturnal; very fast; eats paper and starch

hp: Orthopteran — herbivore or omnivore. Acoustic ecology and vertebrate prey base.

desc: Similar to silverfish but mottled gray-brown and heat-loving. Found near water heaters, ovens, furnaces. Prefers >90°F.

fm.Habitat: Near heat sources — water heaters, ovens

hp: Indoor detritivore — eats starch, paper, textiles, dried food. Near heat sources. Not on plants.

desc: Large shiny dark cockroach called 'waterbug.' Prefers cool damp areas. Being displaced by Turkestan cockroach in SoCal.

fm.Behavior: Slow runner; strong musty smell; declining

hp: Omnivorous scavenger. Detritivore in natural and urban habitats.

desc: Distinctive insect with elongated 'neck' raised cobra-like. A living fossil — unchanged since the Jurassic. Predatory. California endemic genus.

fm.Color: Dark brown-black; elongated prothorax; clear wings
fm.Habitat: Oak woodland — on bark of live oaks
fm.Behavior: Raises front body like a snake; predatory

hp: Generalist predator of insects and other arthropods. Predatory. California.

desc: The most commonly encountered mantis in LA gardens. Smaller than Chinese Mantis. Distinctive purple-blue eyespot on inner hindwing. Introduced from Mediterranean.

fm.Color: Green or brown; purple-blue eyespot on inner HW
fm.Habitat: Gardens, shrubs — the common LA garden mantis

hp: Praying mantis — ambush predator of flying insects. Beneficial garden predator. Garden associate.

desc: Dark wingless earwig with banded yellow-brown legs. Tropical origin. Common in gardens, greenhouses, under pots. More heat-tolerant than European Earwig.

fm.Season: Year-round; more tropical than European Earwig

hp: Indoor trailing ant — nuisance pest. Preys on earwig.

desc: Tiny elongated insect that spins silk from enlarged front tarsi — unique among insects. Lives in silk galleries on tree bark and walls. Colonial. Often overlooked.

fm.Color: Dark brown; soft-bodied; enlarged front feet
fm.Habitat: Tree bark, walls — colonial silk galleries
fm.Behavior: Spins silk from front feet (unique); lives in galleries

hp: Orthopteran — herbivore or omnivore. Acoustic ecology and vertebrate prey base.

desc: Small indoor cockroach preferring dry warm locations (unlike German cockroach). Brown with two light bands across wings. Found in bedrooms, living rooms.

fm.Color: Light brown; two pale transverse bands

hp: Brown-banded cockroach — indoor pest in warm, dry areas of buildings. Prefers higher locations than German cockroach. Scavenger on starchy foods.

desc: Native western earwig — unlike the introduced European earwig, this species has toothed forceps (hence 'dentata'). Found under bark and rocks in foothill habitats.
hp: On tree bark. Part of bark-dwelling community

desc: Invasive South African mantis established in SoCal since ~2014. Smaller and greener than native mantis species. Displacing native Stagmomantis. Females cannibalistic during mating.

fm.Color: Bright green; smaller than native mantises

hp: Ambush predator of flying insects. Beneficial garden predator.

desc: Wheeler's mantis — native praying mantis of desert scrub. Smaller and more slender than Chinese mantis. Tan-brown coloring. Declining in areas colonized by non-native mantises.
hp: Desert and desert-edge habitats. Desert specialist. California.

desc: Large dark wingless earwig of coastal and riparian habitats. Under driftwood and beach debris. Powerful pincers. One of LA County's largest earwigs. Predatory.
hp: Predatory — hunts arthropod prey. Coastal habitat. Preys on earwig.

desc: Very large earwig found on sandy riverbanks and beaches. Up to 1 inch including forceps. Predatory. One of the largest earwigs in the world.
hp: Predator of earwigs and soil-dwelling insects. Preys on earwig. Beach habitat.

desc: Tiny ground-dwelling mantis only 1 inch long. Brown, wingless. Ambushes prey from ground level in sage scrub and grassland. Easily overlooked.
hp: Part of local invertebrate food web. Associated with Black Sage.

desc: Tiny ground-dwelling mantis. Wingless. Cryptic on soil. Extremely well camouflaged. Related to L. pacifica.
hp: Cryptically camouflaged against habitat substrate. Soil-dwelling.

desc: Tiny mantis living on ground in chaparral. Flightless. Brown cryptic coloring matches leaf litter. One of smallest mantises.
hp: In leaf litter. Chaparral habitat. Cryptic camouflage.

desc: Tiny earwig. One of the smallest earwigs. Can fly. On compost heaps and in gardens. Predatory. Mostly nocturnal.

fm.Color: Brown; tiny; can fly — unusual for earwigs

hp: Predator of earwigs and soil-dwelling insects. Preys on earwig. In compost.

desc: Long-necked predatory insect with elongated prothorax giving snake-like appearance. Two-toned brown and black body. Adults and larvae prey on aphids, mites, scale insects, and bark beetle eggs. Found in oak woodlands and chaparral, often on tree trunks. One of several Agulla species in California, distinguishable by coloration and venation.

fm.Diet: Predator — aphids, mites, bark beetle eggs
fm.vs: California Snakefly (A. adnixa) is more uniformly colored

hp: Beneficial predator of bark beetles and aphids in oak woodlands. One of three Agulla species in LA County.
